theory.intro: A survey collects data by asking people questions. Good questions are clear, fair, and match the type of data you want.
facts: **Categorical** data sorts answers into groups (e.g. favourite colour, sport, yes/no).
**Numerical** data is a count or measurement (e.g. number of pets, height in cm).
A good question is **clear**, **unbiased**, and gives **enough answer choices** to cover everyone.
steps: Decide exactly what you want to find out.
Decide if the data will be categorical or numerical.
Write a clear, fair question; check it has no leading wording or missing options.

foundation.q: Will the question 'What is your favourite fruit?' collect categorical or numerical data?
Will the question 'How many books did you read this month?' collect categorical or numerical data?
Will the question 'Which sport do you play?' collect categorical or numerical data?
Will the question 'How tall are you in cm?' collect categorical or numerical data?
Will the question 'Do you own a pet? (yes/no)' collect categorical or numerical data?
Will the question 'How many minutes do you spend on homework?' collect categorical or numerical data?
Write a survey question to find each student's favourite school subject. Is it categorical or numerical?
foundation.a: e.g. 'What is your favourite subject?'; categorical

foundation.q: Write a survey question to find how many siblings each student has. Is it categorical or numerical?
foundation.a: e.g. 'How many siblings do you have?'; numerical

foundation.q: Which question collects numerical data: (A) 'What colour are your eyes?' or (B) 'How many pencils are in your case?'
Which question collects categorical data: (A) 'How old are you?' or (B) 'How do you travel to school?'

development.q: A survey asks 'Don't you agree our canteen is the best?' Why is this a poor question?
development.a: It is leading/biased — it pushes people to agree.

solution.note: e.g. 'How would you rate the canteen?'

development.q: A survey of favourite colours offers only 'Red' and 'Blue'. What is the problem?
development.a: Not enough answer choices — others can't answer.

development.q: Reword the leading question 'Wouldn't you love a longer lunch break?' to be fair.
development.a: e.g. 'How long should the lunch break be?'

development.q: You want to know how students travel to school. Write a clear question and list four answer choices.
development.a: e.g. 'How do you travel to school?' — Walk / Bus / Car / Bike

development.q: Is 'How many cups of water do you drink each day?' a clear question? What data type is it?
A question asks 'What is your favourite junk food or healthy snack?' Why is it unclear?
development.a: It asks two things at once.

development.q: You want numerical data about screen time. Write a suitable question.
development.a: e.g. 'How many hours of screen time do you have on a school day?'

development.q: A survey of pet ownership lists 'Dog, Cat, Fish'. A student owns a rabbit. Improve the choices.
Classify the data type for each: (i) favourite movie, (ii) number of goals scored.
Why might asking 'How fast do you run?' give unreliable survey data?
development.a: It is vague — no units or method given.

mastery.q: You want to find the most popular school sport AND how many hours each student trains. Write one categorical and one numerical question.
mastery.a: e.g. 'Which sport do you play?' (categorical); 'How many hours do you train each week?' (numerical)

mastery.q: A class surveys only the soccer team about favourite sport. Why are the results misleading?
mastery.a: The sample is biased — not representative of the class.

mastery.q: Design a survey to find out which canteen item students want added. State the question, two requirements for fairness, and the data type.
mastery.a: e.g. 'Which new item should the canteen add?'; neutral wording + cover all options; categorical

mastery.q: Rewrite the biased question 'How much do you love our excellent library?' and explain your change.
mastery.a: e.g. 'How would you rate the library?' — removes persuasive words.

mastery.q: A survey asks age as 'Under 10 / 10-12 / Over 10'. Why are these answer groups faulty?
mastery.a: They overlap — a 10-year-old fits two groups.

mastery.q: You want to know both the favourite and least favourite subject. Why should these be two separate questions?
mastery.a: One question can't clearly collect two different answers.

mastery.q: Design a numerical survey question about water bottles and give a sensible set of answer ranges.
mastery.a: e.g. 'How many bottles do you drink daily?' — 0, 1, 2, 3, 4+

mastery.q: Explain why a survey at the school gate at 8 am might miss data on how students travel.
mastery.a: Students arriving early/late or by other means are missed — biased timing.

mastery.q: A survey question gives only two screen-time options: '0 hours' or '10+ hours'. Why is this poor, and how do you fix it?
mastery.a: Most students fit neither; add middle ranges (1-2, 3-4, ...).

mastery.q: Plan a two-question survey to compare boys' and girls' favourite playground games. State both questions and the data types.
mastery.a: e.g. 'Are you a boy or girl?' (categorical); 'What is your favourite playground game?' (categorical)
